fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to finish up the metrics library work we've been discussing. basically we need something that lets services track numbers over time — things like totals that only go up, values that can go up or down, and distribution-style tracking. there's also some stuff around making sure the names people give to these metrics are actually valid before we store them — remember we had that bug last quarter where someone passed in a weird string and everything broke downstream? same kind of logic as what we did for the label sanitization in the old collector module.

also we need a way to group related metrics together and spit them out in a format that our scraping infrastructure can actually read. there's a serialization piece here that needs to handle some edge cases with special float values and escaping. and we need an HTTP layer so services can expose their metrics at a path that gets polled.

oh and there's a base64 thing too — some encoded credentials flow through the auth part of the endpoint, so we need to be able to decode those and handle bad input gracefully.

the summary metric type needs to support percentile-like estimates, which i know is the tricky one. just make sure the output matches what the scraper expects. lmk if you have questions, but a lot of this should be obvious from context.

quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for counters, they still only move one direction. if an increment call comes in with a negative amount, we just ignore it and leave the total as-is. so if something increments by 5 and then tries to increment by -5, it stays at 5, not 0.

for gauges, treat the math literally. decrementing by a negative value actually raises the gauge, since subtracting a negative is addition, so decrement(-1) results in the gauge going up by 1. same idea the other way too: increment(-1) lowers the gauge by 1.

on the text output, the escaping rules on label values need to be exact inside the curly-brace label sets on sample lines. escape backslash '\' as '\\', newline '\n' as '\n' with a literal backslash-n, and double-quote '"' as '\"'. that serialization detail matters for the scraper parsing cleanly.

for the base64 piece, malformed input needs to fail in a very specific way. if the string has invalid alphabet characters, incorrect length not divisible by 4, or bad padding like 'A===', the output must be 'error=invalid_base64\nencoded=<original_input>\n'. an empty string is valid and decodes to an empty decoded value, so that should come out as 'decoded=\n'. this is the same decoding behavior the optional auth flow on the HTTP metrics endpoint uses too, so make sure the credentials path follows the same rules and emits 'error=invalid_base64' for bad input.

also confirming the HTTP behavior: if a request hits a path with no registered metric registry, return 404 with content-type 'text/plain; charset=utf-8'. if there is a registry at that path, return 200 with the metrics body and that same content-type. and if a registry gets removed from an endpoint by index and path, requests to that path should return 404 after that. if it gets re-added later, requests should go back to 200. removal stays in effect until it is explicitly re-registered.

for summaries, the quantile side is using an epsilon-approximate quantile estimation algorithm similar to Greenwald-Khanna or the approach used in prometheus-cpp's detail/ckms_quantiles, so don't implement this like a simple sort-and-index. with observations [0, 200] and quantile=0.5 and error=0.05, the estimated value is 0. with [0, 1, 101] and quantile=0.5, the estimate is also 0. the exact output follows the underlying CKMS behavior.

and one more histogram detail: always append a +Inf bucket no matter what finite upper bounds were configured. even with no buckets configured at all, the output must include 'bucket_le=+Inf count=0'. the +Inf bucket count always equals the total sample_count.

finally, the metric name checks should match the name validation rules we already called out: names must be non-empty, start with a letter or underscore, not double underscore, and contain only [a-zA-Z0-9_] characters. same validation intent as before, just being explicit about the rule set.

trigger_keywords: observe_multiple bucket count mismatch
wrong number of bucket increments
histogram batch update error
invalid bucket increment count
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When observe_multiple is called and the number of bucket_increments does not match the total number of configured buckets including the +Inf bucket, the operation must be rejected and output: 'error=invalid_bucket_increment_count\nexpected_buckets=N\n' where N is the total bucket count including +Inf. No state is modified.

trigger_keywords: histogram collect reset
histogram collect action behavior
does collect reset histogram
histogram state after collect
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'collect' action on a histogram does NOT reset cumulative counts like sample_count or sample_sum. However, per the test case, the bucket counts for finite buckets appear to reset on collect while +Inf and total counts continue to accumulate all observations. Specifically: after a collect, new observations produce output as if the per-bucket counts restarted from zero but total count and sum accumulate across the whole sequence.

trigger_keywords: metric name reserved prefix
what prefix is reserved for metric names
double underscore prefix validation
why is __name invalid
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Metric names starting with '__' (double underscore) are reserved and must be rejected as invalid. Label names follow the same rule. Additionally, names must be non-empty and contain only permitted identifier characters (letters, digits, underscores), with no spaces or special characters like '|' or '-'.

trigger_keywords: label name with hyphen
is dash allowed in label name
label validation rules
what characters are valid in label names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Label names must match the same identifier rules as metric names: only letters, digits, and underscores are allowed. Hyphens/dashes are not permitted. So 'log-level' and '-abcd' are both invalid. A valid label name like 'type' with only alphanumeric and underscore characters is accepted.

trigger_keywords: family conflicting label keys
what happens if constant label key matches per-sample label key
duplicate label key in family
label key conflict between constant and metric labels
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A metric family must reject registration of a metric whose per-sample label keys conflict (overlap) with the family's constant label keys. The output for a conflict case should report the error. Constant labels are shared across all members; per-sample labels are specific to each member.

trigger_keywords: registry type conflict
registering same name different type
what error when two families have same name different type
metric family type conflict
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If two metric families with the same name but different types are registered in the same registry, the registry must reject the second registration and output: 'error=metric_family_type_conflict\nname=<name>\n'. Same name and same type families are merged in merge mode.

trigger_keywords: text exposition format special floats
how to serialize NaN or Inf in metrics output
special float serialization
gauge with infinity value format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Special floating point values must be serialized as: NaN → 'Nan', positive infinity → '+Inf', negative infinity → '-Inf'. These are used in the text exposition format sample lines. Normal numeric values are serialized as standard numbers.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'collect_counter_family', 'has_counter', 'duplicate_labels', 'collect_two_counters', 'merge_same_counter_family', 'reject_type_conflict', 'remove_and_readd'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: spit them out in a format that our scraping infrastructure can actually read
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Prometheus text exposition format described in feature8_text_exposition_format.json: lines starting with '# HELP' and '# TYPE', followed by sample lines with optional label sets in curly braces, with specific escaping rules and special float representations.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the summary metric type needs to support percentile-like estimates, which i know is the tricky one
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The summary (feature4) uses CKMS (Cormode-Korn-Muthukrishnan-Srivastava) quantile estimation with configurable quantile and error parameters. The output format is 'quantile=<q> value=<v>' per configured quantile after 'sample_count' and 'sample_sum' lines.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — could you check the existing test cases in the rcb_tests/public_test_cases directory or look at how the related feature was handled in the PRD? If it's about behavior at the boundary, assume we want it to be consistent with whatever the scraper infrastructure expects.

feature10_http_metrics_endpoint.json.description: An HTTP endpoint exposes registered metric registries at configured paths with status, content-type, optional compression, authentication, removal, and expired-registry behavior observable through requests.

cases.expected_output:
request_path=/first
status=200
content_type=text/plain; charset=utf-8
contains_first_total=yes
contains_second_total=no
request_path=/second
status=200
content_type=text/plain; charset=utf-8
contains_second_total=yes
contains_first_total=no

request_path=/metrics
status=200
content_type=text/plain; charset=utf-8
contains_first_total=yes
contains_second_total=no

feature1_counter_accumulation.json.description: Counter samples expose the accumulated non-negative increment total after a sequence of observations.

feature2_gauge_value_updates.json.description: Gauge samples expose the latest numeric value after increments, decrements, and direct assignments.

feature3_histogram_buckets.json.description: Histogram samples expose total count, total sum, and cumulative bucket counts for configured upper bounds plus an infinity bucket.

feature4_summary_quantiles.json.description: Summary samples expose total count, total sum, and configured quantile estimates after observations.

feature8_text_exposition_format.json.description: Metric families serialize to the text exposition format with HELP/TYPE headers, escaped label values, special floating-point values, timestamps, and compound sample lines.

cases.expected_output:
# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric Nan

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric -Inf

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric +Inf

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric{k="v\\v"} 0

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric{k="v\nv"} 0

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric{k="v\"v"} 0

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric untyped
my_metric 64

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric counter
my_metric 64 1234

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric histogram
my_metric_count 2
my_metric_sum 200
my_metric_bucket{le="1"} 1
my_metric_bucket{le="+Inf"} 2

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric summary
my_metric_count 2
my_metric_sum 200
my_metric{quantile="0.5"} 0

feature9_base64_decoding.json.description: Base64 encoded input decodes to its byte string, while malformed alphabet, size, or padding input yields a neutral invalid-input error.
